summary: This first Book of Moses doth contain the history of all things from the Creation of heaven and earth, wherein the Lord God made man in His own image and placed him in Eden, yet through the subtlety of the serpent our first parents did eat of the forbidden tree and were cast forth. Then followeth the murder of Abel by Cain, the long lives of the Patriarchs before the Flood, and the building of Noah's ark, whereby eight souls were saved when the waters destroyed all flesh. After the Deluge, the nations were dispersed at Babel, and the Lord called Abraham from Ur, promising that in his seed all nations should be blessed; and though Abraham and Sarah were old, Isaac was born, whom the father was commanded to offer upon Moriah, but stayed by the angel's hand. Isaac begat Jacob, who by guile obtained the birthright, and after wrestling with God was named Israel; his twelve sons became the heads of the twelve tribes, and of Joseph, who was sold into Egypt, made lord over all the land, and who saved Egypt and his brethren from famine. Joseph died in Egypt at an hundred and ten years, charging that his bones be carried thence.

This Book recounteth how the children of Israel did multiply in Egypt and were brought into sore bondage under a new Pharaoh, who knew not Joseph; and how Moses, drawn from the bulrushes and brought up in Pharaoh's house, fled to Midian after slaying an Egyptian. There the Lord appeared to him in a burning bush, commanding him to lead His people forth; and after ten grievous plagues, the last being the death of the firstborn, Pharaoh let them go. The Lord divided the Red Sea before them and drowned the Egyptians therein. In the wilderness the Lord gave manna and water from the rock, and upon Mount Sinai delivered the Ten Commandments engraved with the finger of God. Moses received the pattern of the Tabernacle, the Ark, and the priestly garments; and when the people sinned in making a golden calf, Moses interceded for them. At the last, the Tabernacle was reared and the glory of the Lord filled it, and the cloud led them by day and fire by night.

Herein are set forth the laws which the Lord spake unto Moses out of the Tabernacle, concerning the divers offerings: burnt offerings, meat offerings, peace offerings, sin offerings, and trespass offerings, with the manner of their sacrifice by the priesthood of Aaron and his sons. The consecration of the priests is declared, and the great Day of Atonement, wherein the high priest alone entereth within the veil with blood for the sins of all Israel. The book speaketh also of clean and unclean beasts, of leprosy and its purification, of issues and uncleanness, and of the holiness required of the people, saying, "Ye shall be holy, for I the Lord your God am holy." The sabbaths and feasts are appointed: the sabbath, the passover, the feast of weeks, the feast of trumpets, the day of atonement, and the feast of tabernacles; with laws concerning blasphemy, and the year of jubilee, when every man returneth to his possession.

This Book taketh its name from the numbering of the children of Israel in the wilderness, when the tribes were mustered by their thousands for war. The order of the camp is set forth, and the charge of the Levites about the Tabernacle. Yet the people murmured against the Lord, and fire burned among them, and Miriam and Aaron spake against Moses; the ten spies brought an evil report of Canaan, wherefore that generation was condemned to wander forty years, save Caleb and Joshua. Korah and his company rebelled and were swallowed of the earth. Miriam died at Kadesh, and Moses smote the rock in anger and was forbidden to enter the promised land. Balaam's ass spake, and notwithstanding he blessed Israel, yet Israel fell into idolatry with the daughters of Moab. Then the daughters of Zelophehad pleaded for their inheritance, and Moses viewed the land from mount Nebo before his death.

These be the words which Moses spake unto all Israel in the plain over against the Red sea, in the fortieth year, repeating the Law before his death; he recounted the Lord's deliverance from Egypt, the giving of the Commandments at Horeb, and the Lord's chastisements in the wilderness. He charged them to love the Lord with all their heart and soul, to destroy the Canaanites and their idols utterly, and not to add nor diminish from the Law. The blessings for obedience and the curses for disobedience are pronounced with terrible solemnity upon mount Ebal and Gerizim. Moses wrote the Law in a book and gave charge to Joshua, and sang a song unto the Lord, blessing the tribes. Then the Lord shewed him the land from the top of Pisgah, and Moses died there in Moab at an hundred and twenty years, full of wisdom, and no man knoweth his sepulchre unto this day.

After the death of Moses, the Lord spake unto Joshua, Moses' minister, saying, "Be strong and of a good courage," and Joshua led the people over Jordan, the waters being cut off as before when Israel passed through the Red Sea. The walls of Jericho fell down flat at the sound of the trumpets of rams' horns; but Achan took of the accursed thing and was stoned, and Ai was taken in ambush. The sun stood still upon Gibeon at Joshua's word, and the five kings were slain. The land was divided by lot among the nine tribes and an half, with Reuben, Gad, and half Manasseh having their portion on the other side of Jordan. The cities of refuge were appointed for the manslayer, and the Levites had their cities. Joshua made a covenant with the people at Shechem, saying, "Choose you this day whom ye will serve; but as for me and my house, we will serve the Lord." He died at an hundred and ten years.

This Book containeth the history of Israel after the death of Joshua, when the people served the Lord whilst the elders lived, but afterward fell away to idols; and the Lord delivered them into the hands of their enemies, then raised up judges to deliver them. Othniel, Ehud who slew Eglon, Deborah and Barak who overcame Sisera at Megiddo, Gideon who with three hundred men routed the Midianites; yet Gideon made an ephod which became a snare. Abimelech slew his brethren, Jephthah vowed rashly, and Samson, a Nazarite of marvellous strength, slew a thousand Philistines with the jawbone of an ass, but was betrayed by Delilah, his eyes put out, and died pulling down the house upon the lords of the Philistines. In those days there was no king, and every man did that which was right in his own eyes, as in the matter of the Levite's concubine and the war against Benjamin.

In the days when the judges ruled, there was a famine in Judah, and Elimelech of Bethlehem went to Moab with his wife Naomi and his two sons, who took Moabitish wives, Orpah and Ruth; and Elimelech and his sons died. Naomi returned to Bethlehem, and Ruth clave unto her, saying, "Whither thou goest, I will go; thy people shall be my people, and thy God my God." Ruth gleaned in the fields of Boaz, who was a kinsman of Elimelech, and Naomi directed her to claim the right of the kinsman-redeemer. Boaz took Ruth to wife, and she bare a son, Obed, the father of Jesse, the father of David. This moabtitish woman, through her faith and constancy, was counted worthy to be in the lineage of our blessed Lord.

Otherwise called the First Book of the Kings, it telleth how Hannah prayed for a son at Shiloh, and bare Samuel, whom she gave to the Lord. The sons of Eli, Hophni and Phinehas, were wicked priests, and the ark was taken by the Philistines, who returned it upon the plague of emerods. Samuel judged Israel, and the people demanded a king; the Lord gave them Saul, son of Kish, who began well but disobeyed by sparing Agag and the best of the Amalekites. Samuel anointed David, the son of Jesse, a shepherd lad, whom the Lord chose above his elder brethren; and David slew Goliath the Philistine with a sling and a stone. Saul grew jealous and pursueth David, yet spared him twice in the cave and at Hachilah. The Philistines slew Saul and Jonathan at mount Gilboa, and the men of Jabeshgilead recovered their bodies.

The Second Book of the Kings continueth the reign of David, who reigned first over Judah at Hebron, and then over all Israel. He took Jerusalem, made it his city, and brought the ark thither with dancing. The Lord promised that of his seed the throne should be established for ever. David sinned grievously with Bathsheba the wife of Uriah, and caused Uriah to be slain in battle; Nathan the prophet declared, "Thou art the man," and though David repented, the sword never departed from his house. Amnon ravished Tamar, Absalom slew Amnon and rebelled, driving David from Jerusalem; Absalom was slain by Joab, and David mourned bitterly. Sheba the son of Bichri made insurrection but was beheaded. The book endeth with David's sin in numbering the people, the plague thereupon, and the threshingfloor of Araunah which he bought for the building of an altar.

Commonly called the Third Book of the Kings, it beginneth with the aged David, and Adonijah's usurpation; Solomon was anointed and David charged him to build the house of the Lord. Solomon asked the Lord for wisdom, and he judged wisely between the two harlots, and his wisdom and riches excelled all. He built the Temple at Jerusalem with cedar of Lebanon and gold, and the glory of the Lord filled the house; he dedicated it with prayer and sacrifice. Yet Solomon loved many strange women and built high places for their gods, wherefore the Lord rent the kingdom from his son. After Solomon's death, Rehoboam refused to lighten the yoke, and ten tribes followed Jeroboam. Jeroboam made two golden calves and erected idolatrous worship. Elijah the Tishbite prophesied against Ahab and Jezebel, stayed the rain three years and six months, and slew the prophets of Baal upon Carmel in the fire from heaven; he was fed by ravens and sustained by the widow of Zarephath.

The Fourth Book of the Kings relateth how Ahaziah fell through a lattice and sent to Baalzebub, and Elijah called down fire upon the captains; then Elijah was taken up to heaven in a chariot of fire, and Elisha received a double portion of his spirit. Elisha made the iron swim, cured Naaman the Syrian of his leprosy, and raised the Shunammite's son. Jehu slew Jezebel and the house of Ahab, destroying Baal out of Israel, yet followed the sins of Jeroboam. The kings of Israel and Judah walked mostly in iniquity; Hoshea was the last king of Israel, and the Assyrians carried the ten tribes captive. In Judah, Hezekiah cleansed the temple and trusted the Lord, and Isaiah prayed, and the shadow returned ten degrees on the dial of Ahaz. Manasseh wrought much wickedness, and Josiah found the book of the Law and kept a passover such as had not been kept. Nebuchadnezzar took Jerusalem, burned the temple, and carried the people to Babylon.

This Book repeateth the genealogies from Adam to the sons of Israel, with the generations of the tribes, the Levites, and the sons of David. It telleth of the death of Saul and his sons upon mount Gilboa, and how David's men fetched the ark from Kirjathjearim with great rejoicing, though Uzza was smitten for touching it. David prepared a place for the ark in Jerusalem, and set over the singers, the porters, and the ministers of the Levites; he subdued the Philistines, the Moabites, and other nations, and took much spoil. The Lord made a covenant with David that his house and kingdom should be established for ever. David made great preparation of gold and silver, brass and iron, and timber and stone, for the building of the temple which Solomon should build, and charged the princes and the people to seek the commandments of the Lord.

It continueth from Solomon's accession, when he went to Gibeon and the Lord appeared to him in a dream, giving him wisdom and riches; and Solomon built the house of the Lord and his own palace, dedicated the temple, and the glory of the Lord filled it. The queen of Sheba came to hear his wisdom. After Solomon's death, the kingdom was rent; Rehoboam fortified the cities of Judah, yet Shishak king of Egypt came against him because they had forsaken the Lord. Asa trusted God and overcame the Ethiopians, Jehoshaphat sent Levites to teach the Law, and the Lord gave great victory over Ammon and Moab. Hezekiah cleansed the temple and restored the passover, and the Lord saved Jerusalem from Sennacherib's army. Josiah kept a great passover; after his death the land was given into the hand of Nebuchadnezzar, and the temple was burned, the vessels carried away, and the people captive until the land had enjoyed her sabbaths, threescore and ten years.

In the first year of Cyrus king of Persia, the Lord stirred up his spirit that he made a proclamation saying, "The Lord God of heaven hath given me all the kingdoms of the earth; and He hath charged me to build Him an house at Jerusalem." Then went up Zerubbabel and Jeshua with the people, and the vessels of the temple were restored. When the adversaries sought to build with them and were refused, they hindered the work until the second year of Darius. Then the prophets Haggai and Zechariah stirred them up, and the work went forward, and Darius confirmed Cyrus's decree. Ezra the priest and scribe, skilled in the law of Moses, went up from Babylon with a commission from Artaxerxes to beautify the house of the Lord, to teach His statutes and judgments in Israel. He mourned because the people had taken strange wives, and a great assembly was held to put them away, and to separate themselves from the uncleanness of the heathen.

Nehemiah, the son of Hachaliah, being cupbearer to Artaxerxes the king, heard of the desolation of Jerusalem and wept; and the king granted him letters to go up and build the wall. He surveyed the ruins by night, and encouraging the people, they built with one hand and held a weapon in the other, for the adversaries Sanballat and Tobiah mocked and threatened them. The wall was finished in fifty and two days, and the enemies were cast down. Then Ezra read the Law before the congregation, and the Levites expounded it; and the people wept, but were charged to rejoice. They sealed a covenant to walk in God's law, to observe the sabbaths and the ordinances; the inhabitants of Jerusalem were numbered, the priests and Levites appointed their courses, and the dedication of the wall was celebrated with thanksgivings, singing, and great gladness, for God had turned their mourning into joy.

In the days of Ahasuerus, king of Persia, who reigned from India to Ethiopia, he made a feast the glory whereof was exceeding great; and Vashti the queen refusing to come at his command, was put away. Then fair young virgins were gathered, and Esther, a Jewess of the tribe of Benjamin, the cousin of Mordecai, was chosen queen above all. Haman the Agagite, being advanced above the princes, sought to destroy all the Jews throughout the empire because Mordecai would not bow to him; by lot the day was set, and the decree went forth. Mordecai rent his clothes, and Esther, fasting three days, went in unto the king unbidden, and he held out the golden sceptre. She invited the king and Haman to a banquet; that night the king read how Mordecai had saved him from the conspiracy, and Haman was forced to honour him. Haman was hanged on the gallows he had prepared for Mordecai, and Mordecai and Esther obtained letters for the Jews to defend themselves, so that upon the thirteenth day of Adar their enemies fell, and it was ordained as the feast of Purim.

There was a man in the land of Uz named Job, perfect and upright, fearing God and eschewing evil, with great substance and ten children; and the Lord permitted Satan to try him. In one day Job lost his oxen, asses, sheep, camels, servants, and all his children by a great wind; yet he blessed God. Satan smote him with sore boils from head to foot, and his wife said, "Curse God, and die," but he replied, "Shall we receive good at the hand of God, and shall we not receive evil?" Three friends, Eliphaz, Bildad, and Zophar, came to comfort him and argued that his afflictions must be for his sins; Elihu spake with burning anger that the friends condemned Job without cause, yet Job was too righteous in his own eyes. Then the Lord answered Job out of the whirlwind, declaring His power in creation and dominion over Behemoth and Leviathan; Job repented in dust and ashes, and the Lord restored him twice as much as he had before, and he saw his sons' sons to four generations.

This Book containeth a hundred and fifty psalms of David and other holy men, being the songs and prayers of the Church, wherein are set forth all the affections of the godly soul: praises to the Lord for His creation, providence, and deliverance; penitent cries for pardon, as the fifty-first, "Have mercy upon me, O God, according to thy lovingkindness"; confidence in the Lord's protection, as the twenty-third, "The Lord is my shepherd"; the blessings of the righteous and the fall of the wicked; the glory of God's law, of Zion, and of the Messiah's kingdom. The coming of Christ is foretold: His suffering, as in the twenty-second, "They pierced my hands and my feet," and His exaltation, as in the second and hundred and tenth, where he sitteth at the right hand of God. It is a book of infinite comfort and instruction, teaching us to bless the Lord at all times, and showing that judgment shall be executed upon the ungodly and salvation upon those that trust in Him.

The proverbs of Solomon the son of David, king of Israel, written to give wisdom and instruction to the simple and the young; wisdom crieth aloud in the streets, saying, "How long, ye simple ones, will ye love simplicity?" The fear of the Lord is the beginning of knowledge. Herein are set forth the blessings of righteousness and the destruction that waiteth upon the wicked, the froward tongue, the slothful hand, the false witness, and the adulteress whose house descendeth to death. The instruction of a father to a son, the praise of a virtuous woman whose price is far above rubies, the value of a soft answer that turneth away wrath, the danger of wine and strong drink, and the contrast between the fool and the wise man, the diligent and the sluggard, are plainly declared. The Lord hateth a proud look, a lying tongue, and hands that shed innocent blood; but mercy and truth are the delight of the righteous.

The words of the Preacher, the son of David, king in Jerusalem: "Vanity of vanities, saith the Preacher, vanity of vanities; all is vanity." He declareth the weariness of all human labour and the unprofitableness of seeking pleasure in wisdom, in riches, in mirth, and in wine, for the wise man and the fool die alike. There is a season for all things under the heaven: a time to be born and a time to die, a time to plant and a time to pluck up. He considered the oppression under the sun, and that two are better than one, because they have a good reward for their labour. Remember thy Creator in the days of thy youth, before the evil days come; for the dust returneth to the earth as it was, and the spirit returneth unto God who gave it. The conclusion of the whole matter is, "Fear God, and keep His commandments: for this is the whole duty of man; for God shall bring every work into judgment, with every secret thing, whether it be good, or whether it be evil."

The Song of songs, which is Solomon's, being a mystical allegory of Christ and His Church, wherein the Bridegroom and the Bride express their love for one another in the language of the eastern nations. "Let him kiss me with the kisses of his mouth: for thy love is better than wine." She seeketh Him whom her soul loveth, and the watchmen found her; He calleth her fair, saying, "Thou art all fair, my love; there is no spot in thee." She saith of Him, "My beloved is mine, and I am his; he feedeth among the lilies." The daughters of Jerusalem are adjured not to stir up love until it please. It setteth forth the excellency of love which is strong as death, and jealousy which is cruel as the grave, and the excellency of the Beloved in His head of fine gold, His eyes as doves by the water brooks, His lips as lilies dropping sweet-smelling myrrh, and His countenance as Lebanon.

The vision which Isaiah the son of Amoz saw in the days of Uzziah, Jotham, Ahaz, and Hezekiah, concerning Judah and Jerusalem; the book beginneth with the Lord's complaint that His people are a sinful nation, laden with iniquity, and that He will thoroughly purge away the dross. The prophet foretelleth the invasion of the Assyrians and the captivity by Babylon, yet comforteth the people with the promise of the Messiah: "For unto us a child is born, unto us a son is given: and the government shall be upon his shoulder." He describeth the suffering servant who was wounded for our transgressions, bruised for our iniquities, led as a lamb to the slaughter, and buried with the rich. He prophesieth the fall of Babylon, the return of the captives, and the new heavens and new earth wherein the wolf and the lamb shall feed together. Woe is pronounced against the proud, the drunkards of Ephraim, and those that call evil good and good evil; but a remnant shall be saved.

The words of Jeremiah the son of Hilkiah, whom the Lord called from the womb to be a prophet unto the nations; he prophesied in the days of Josiah, Jehoiakim, and Zedekiah, warning Judah of the calamity that should come from the north because of their idolatry and forsaking of the covenant. He spake of the broken cisterns, the barren fig tree, and the potter's vessel marred in the hand of the potter. He prophesied seventy years of captivity in Babylon, and wrote to the captives that they should build houses and pray for the peace of the city. He foretold the new covenant which the Lord would make with the house of Israel, putting His law in their inward parts and writing it in their hearts. The princes cast him into a dungeon of mire, yet he was delivered. He watched the destruction of Jerusalem, the burning of the temple, and the carrying away of the remnant into Egypt, yet comforted them that they should yet return and build.

How doth the city sit solitary, that was full of people! she that was great among the nations is become as a widow. These are the lamentations of Jeremiah over the destruction of Jerusalem, when the Lord covered the daughter of Zion with a cloud in His anger, and cast down from heaven unto the earth the beauty of Israel. He hath set me in dark places, as they that be dead of old; against me hath He turned His hand all the day. The tender mercies of the Lord are not consumed, for His compassions fail not; they are new every morning: great is Thy faithfulness. It is good that a man should both hope and quietly wait for the salvation of the Lord. For the Lord will not cast off for ever: but though He cause grief, yet will He have compassion according to the multitude of His mercies. The punishment of the iniquity of the daughter of Zion is accomplished; the Lord hath commanded concerning Jacob that they that be about him should be his adversaries.

The visions of Ezekiel the priest by the river Chebar in the captivity; he saw the likeness of the glory of the Lord in a great cloud and fire, with four living creatures and wheels full of eyes; and he was commanded to eat the roll, and to speak unto a rebellious house. He acted out the siege of Jerusalem by lying on his side for many days; the glory departed from the temple because of the abominations, the images at the door, and the women weeping for Tammuz. Yet the Lord promised that He would search for His sheep as a shepherd seeketh his flock, and that He would give them one shepherd, His servant David. The dry bones in the valley lived when the prophet prophesied, bone to his bone, a great army testifying that the Lord shall open the graves and bring His people into the land of Israel. He shewed the pattern of a new temple, the waters issuing therefrom to heal the dead sea, and the land divided by lot among the tribes in the latter days.

In the third year of Jehoiakim, Daniel was carried to Babylon with the princes of Judah, and he and his companions refused the king's meat and wine, being nourished with pulse. He interpreted Nebuchadnezzar's dream of the great image of gold, silver, brass, iron, and clay, and the stone that smote it. Shadrach, Meshach, and Abednego were cast into the fiery furnace for not worshipping the golden image, yet the Son of God walked with them in the fire. Belshazzar saw the handwriting on the wall, "MENE, MENE, TEKEL, UPHARSIN," and Daniel interpreted that the kingdom was numbered and finished. Daniel was cast into the den of lions for praying to his God, but the Lord shut the lions' mouths, and Darius decreed that all should tremble before the God of Daniel. Daniel saw the vision of the four beasts and the Ancient of days; the Son of man coming with the clouds of heaven; the seventy weeks determined upon the people, and the end of desolations.

The word of the Lord came to Hosea the son of Beeri in the days of Uzziah and Hezekiah; the Lord commanded him to take a wife of whoredoms, even Gomer, as a figure of Israel's spiritual adultery. The prophet declared, "They have sown the wind, and they shall reap the whirlwind," for Israel had gone a whoring after Baal and the calves of Bethel. The Lord pleadeth with His people: "How shall I give thee up, Ephraim? how shall I deliver thee, Israel? mine heart is turned within me." They shall return and seek the Lord their God and David their king in the latter days. "O Israel, return unto the Lord thy God; for thou hast fallen by thine iniquity. Take with you words, and turn to the Lord: say unto him, Take away all iniquity." The Lord will be as the dew unto Israel, and he shall grow as the lily, and cast forth his roots as Lebanon.

The word of the Lord that came to Joel the son of Pethuel, concerning a plague of locusts which had eaten the land bare, a day of darkness and of gloominess; the drunkards are to howl for the wine is cut off, and the priests are to mourn for the meat offering and the drink offering is withholden from the house of the Lord. "Rend your heart, and not your garments, and turn unto the Lord your God: for he is gracious and merciful, slow to anger, and of great kindness." He prophesieth that in the last days the Lord will pour out His Spirit upon all flesh, and the sons and daughters shall prophesy, the old men dream dreams, and the young men see visions; and wonders in heaven and earth, blood and fire and pillars of smoke. The sun shall be turned into darkness and the moon into blood before the great and terrible day of the Lord come; but whosoever shall call on the name of the Lord shall be delivered.

The words of Amos, a herdman of Tekoa, which he saw concerning Israel in the days of Jeroboam the son of Joash; he declared against Damascus, Gaza, Tyrus, Edom, and Ammon for their transgressions, and against Judah and Israel. "Let judgment run down as waters, and righteousness as a mighty stream." Israel loved the day of the Lord, but it shall be darkness; they sold the righteous for silver and the poor for a pair of shoes. The Lord shewed Amos a plumbline, signifying He would not pass by them any more; and a basket of summer fruit, signifying the end was come. Yet the Lord promised, "In that day will I raise up the tabernacle of David that is fallen, and close up the breaches thereof; and I will plant them upon their land, and they shall no more be pulled up out of their land which I have given them, saith the Lord thy God."

The vision of Obadiah concerning Edom: We have heard a rumour from the Lord that He will make thee small among the heathen. The pride of thine heart hath deceived thee, thou that dwellest in the clefts of the rock, that saith in his heart, Who shall bring me down to the ground? Though thou exalt thyself as the eagle, thence will I bring thee down, saith the Lord. In the day that thou stoodest on the other side, when strangers carried away the captive forces of Jerusalem, thou shouldest not have looked on their affliction, nor have laid hands on their substance. For the day of the Lord is near upon all the heathen; as thou hast done, it shall be done unto thee. Saviours shall come up on mount Zion to judge the mount of Esau; and the kingdom shall be the Lord's.

Now the word of the Lord came unto Jonah the son of Amittai, saying, Arise, go to Nineveh, that great city, and cry against it; but Jonah fled unto Tarshish. The Lord sent a great wind into the sea, and when the mariners cast lots it fell upon Jonah; he told them, "I fear the Lord, the God of heaven, which hath made the sea and the dry land," and they cast him into the sea, and the sea ceased from her raging. The Lord prepared a great fish to swallow him, and Jonah was in the belly three days and three nights, and prayed, "Salvation is of the Lord." The fish cast him out upon the dry land, and he went to Nineveh and cried, "Yet forty days, and Nineveh shall be overthrown." The people fasted, from the greatest to the least, and God repented of the evil; but Jonah was displeased, and the Lord corrected him by the token of the gourd that withered, teaching that He is merciful and spareth the penitent.

The word of the Lord that came to Micah the Morasthite in the days of Jotham, Ahaz, and Hezekiah, which he saw concerning Samaria and Jerusalem; for the transgression of Jacob is all this, and for the sins of the house of Israel. They covet fields, and take them by violence, through whose commandment the prophets divine for money. "But thou, Bethlehem Ephratah, though thou be little among the thousands of Judah, yet out of thee shall He come forth unto me that is to be ruler in Israel; whose goings forth have been from of old, from everlasting." He hath shewed thee, O man, what is good; and what doth the Lord require of thee, but to do justly, and to love mercy, and to walk humbly with thy God? The Lord pardoneth iniquity, He retaineth not His anger for ever, because He delighteth in mercy; He will subdue our iniquities, and cast all their sins into the depths of the sea.

The burden of Nineveh, the book of the vision of Nahum the Elkoshite: God is jealous, and the Lord revengeth; the Lord revengeth, and is furious; He reserveth wrath for His enemies. The Lord hath His way in the whirlwind and in the storm, and the clouds are the dust of His feet. Nineveh is of old like a pool of water, yet they flee away; the gates of the rivers are opened, and the palace is dissolved. The horseman lifteth up the bright sword and the glittering spear, and there is a multitude of slain. Because of the multitude of the whoredoms of the wellfavoured harlot, the mistress of witchcrafts, that selleth nations through her whoredoms, behold, I am against thee, saith the Lord of hosts. There is no healing of thy bruise; all that hear the bruit of thee shall clap the hands over thee.

The burden which Habakkuk the prophet did see: O Lord, how long shall I cry, and Thou wilt not hear! He complaineth that the law is slacked and judgment doth never go forth, for the wicked doth compass about the righteous; and the Lord answered that He would raise up the Chaldeans, that bitter and hasty nation, to march through the breadth of the land to possess the dwellingplaces that are not theirs. "The just shall live by his faith." Woe unto him that increaseth that which is not his, that ladeth himself with thick clay, and buildeth a town by blood and iniquity. Woe unto him that giveth his neighbour drink; the cup of the Lord's right hand shall be turned unto thee. For the earth shall be filled with the knowledge of the glory of the Lord, as the waters cover the sea. Yet I will rejoice in the Lord, I will joy in the God of my salvation; the Lord God is my strength.

The word of the Lord which came to Zephaniah the son of Cushi in the days of Josiah: I will utterly consume all things from off the land, and I will stretch out Mine hand upon Judah and upon all the inhabitants of Jerusalem, and I will cut off the remnant of Baal, and the name of the Chemarims with the priests; those that worship the host of heaven upon the housetops. The great day of the Lord is near; it is near, and hasteth greatly, even the voice of the day of the Lord; a day of wrath, a day of trouble and distress. Gather yourselves together, yea, gather together, O nation not desired; seek ye the Lord, all ye meek of the earth. "I will also leave in the midst of thee an afflicted and poor people, and they shall trust in the name of the Lord." The Lord thy God in the midst of thee is mighty; He will save, He will rejoice over thee with joy; He will rest in His love, He will joy over thee with singing.

In the second year of Darius, the word of the Lord came by Haggai the prophet unto Zerubbabel the governor, and Joshua the high priest, saying, This people say, The time is not come that the Lord's house should be built, yet they dwell in cieled houses; consider your ways. Ye have sown much and bring in little. Go up to the mountain, and bring wood, and build the house; and I will take pleasure in it, and I will be glorified, saith the Lord. The silver is Mine, and the gold is Mine. Yet once, it is a little while, and I will shake the heavens, and the earth, and the sea, and the dry land; and I will shake all nations, and the desire of all nations shall come, and I will fill this house with glory. From this day will I bless thee. In that day, saith the Lord of hosts, will I take thee, O Zerubbabel, My servant, and will make thee as a signet: for I have chosen thee.

The word of the Lord came to Zechariah the son of Berechiah in the second year of Darius, calling the people to turn from their evil ways, and shewing him visions: the horses among the myrtle trees, the four horns, the man with a measuring line, Joshua the high priest standing before the angel with the filthy garments, and the candlestick with seven lamps and two olive trees. He prophesied of the Branch; of Jerusalem inhabited as towns without walls for the multitude of men and cattle, with the Lord a wall of fire round about; and that they shall look upon Him whom they have pierced, and mourn. He foretold the smiting of the shepherd and the scattering of the sheep, the purification of the land, the battle of Jerusalem, and the Lord's feet standing upon the mount of Olives; and that living waters shall go out from Jerusalem, and the Lord shall be King over all the earth.

The burden of the word of the Lord to Israel by Malachi; He declared, I have loved you, yet ye say, Wherein hast thou loved us? Was not Esau Jacob's brother? yet I loved Jacob, and hated Esau. The priests have corrupted the covenant of Levi, offering polluted bread and blemished sacrifices, saying, Wherein have we wearied Him? In that ye say, Every one that doeth evil is good in the sight of the Lord. The Lord hateth putting away; cover not violence with thy garment. Behold, I will send my messenger, and he shall prepare the way before me; and the Lord, whom ye seek, shall suddenly come to His temple. But who may abide the day of His coming? for He is like a refiner's fire. Bring ye all the tithes into the storehouse, and prove me now, saith the Lord of hosts, if I will not open you the windows of heaven. Unto you that fear my name shall the Sun of righteousness arise with healing in His wings.

The Gospel according to Saint Matthew; it beginneth with the genealogy of Jesus Christ, the son of David, the son of Abraham, and telleth how He was born of Mary by the Holy Ghost while she was espoused to Joseph the carpenter. The wise men came from the east, and Herod slew the infants of Bethlehem; the holy family fled to Egypt. Jesus was baptized of John in Jordan, and the Spirit descended as a dove and the voice from heaven said, This is my beloved Son. He was tempted forty days in the wilderness, and preached, "Repent, for the kingdom of heaven is at hand." He gave the Sermon on the Mount with the beatitudes, taught in parables of the sower, the wheat and tares, the pearl of great price, and the net; He raised the dead, stilled the tempest, and walked upon the sea; Peter confessed Him the Christ. He entered Jerusalem on the colt, cleansed the temple, prophesied of the last days, was betrayed by Judas, crucified, and on the third day rose again, saying, All power is given unto me in heaven and in earth.

The Gospel according to Saint Mark; it is the briefest account of the ministry of Jesus Christ, the Son of God, beginning with the preaching of John the Baptist in the wilderness, the baptism of Jesus, and His temptation. Straightway Jesus preached the gospel, called the fishermen to be fishers of men, healed the sick, and cast out devils; He taught in parables, walked upon the water, and fed five thousand with five loaves and two fishes. He asked the disciples, "Whom do men say that I am?" and Peter answered, Thou art the Christ. He was transfigured before Peter, James, and John, and came down healing the lunatic. He entered Jerusalem, drove the buyers and sellers from the temple, and foretold the destruction thereof. The woman anointed His feet, He kept the passover, was betrayed by Judas, denied by Peter, crucified between two thieves, buried, and rose the third day, and appeared first to Mary Magdalene; and they went forth preaching everywhere, the Lord working with them.

The Gospel according to Saint Luke, written to Theophilus, of perfect understanding from the very first; it telleth of the birth of John the Baptist to Zacharias and Elisabeth, and the annunciation to Mary that she should bear the Son of the Highest. Jesus was born at Bethlehem, laid in a manger, and the angels sang glory to God in the highest; He was presented in the temple and grew in wisdom and stature. He preached in Nazareth that the Scripture was fulfilled in their ears, healed the centurion's servant, raised the widow's son, and stilled the storm. He sent the twelve, and afterwards seventy, and told the parable of the Good Samaritan who shewed mercy upon him that fell among thieves, and of the prodigal son who was received by his father when he returned. He blessed the little children, wept over Jerusalem, and kept the last passover, saying, This is my body which is given for you. He was crucified for us, and on the first day of the week He appeared, and was carried up into heaven.

The Gospel according to Saint John; it openeth with the most sublime declaration, "In the beginning was the Word, and the Word was with God, and the Word was God." John bare witness of the Light; Jesus turned water into wine at Cana, talked with Nicodemus of the new birth, and gave living water to the woman of Samaria. He healed the impotent man at Bethesda, fed five thousand, walked on the sea, and declared, "I am the bread of life," "I am the light of the world," "Before Abraham was, I am." He raised Lazarus from the dead after four days; Mary anointed His feet; He washed the disciples' feet at the passover, and spake the greater discourses, promising the Comforter. He was betrayed, examined before Annas, Pilate, and crucified; His side was pierced, and He gave up the ghost, that the Scripture might be fulfilled. He rose and shewed Himself to Mary, to the ten, to Thomas, and to the disciples by the sea; and John testified that these things are written that ye might believe that Jesus is the Christ, the Son of God.

The Acts of the Apostles, written to Theophilus as the former treatise's continuation; Jesus shewed Himself forty days after His passion, speaking of the kingdom of God, and was taken up while the angels stood by. At Pentecost the Holy Ghost descended in tongues of fire, and Peter preached that God hath made that same Jesus, whom ye have crucified, both Lord and Christ; three thousand were baptized. The lame man was healed at the Beautiful gate; Stephen was stoned as the first martyr; Philip baptized the Ethiopian eunuch; Saul was converted on the road to Damascus, and became Paul, the apostle to the Gentiles. Peter preached to Cornelius the centurion, and the Gentiles received the Holy Ghost. Paul with Barnabas and Silas journeyed through Asia and Europe, establishing churches; at Philippi the jailer was converted. Paul reasoned at Athens upon Mars' hill, and at Ephesus the books of curious arts were burned. After many perils and shipwreck he came to Rome and dwelt two years in his own hired house, preaching the kingdom of God with all confidence.

The Epistle of Paul the Apostle to the Romans; Paul, a servant of Jesus Christ, declared the gospel of God, which He had promised by the prophets; it is the power of God unto salvation to every one that believeth. All have sinned and come short of the glory of God, but being justified freely by His grace through the redemption that is in Christ Jesus, whom God hath set forth to be a propitiation through faith in His blood. Abraham believed God, and it was counted unto him for righteousness; therefore we conclude that a man is justified by faith without the deeds of the law. The Spirit itself beareth witness that we are the children of God; and if God be for us, who can be against us? He that spared not His own Son, shall He not with Him also freely give us all things? Therefore brethren, present your bodies a living sacrifice, holy, acceptable unto God, being not conformed to this world, but transformed by the renewing of your mind.

The First Epistle of Paul the Apostle to the Corinthians; he wrote unto the church of God at Corinth, which was sanctified in Christ Jesus, thanking God that in everything they were enriched. He rebuked their divisions, for some said, I am of Paul, and I of Apollos, and I of Cephas; yet Paul planted and Apollos watered, but God gave the increase. He spake of the foolishness of the cross, which to the Greeks is foolishness, but unto us which are saved it is the power of God. He corrected them for the man that had his father's wife, for going to law before the unjust, and for eating things sacrificed to idols. He delivered the institution of the Lord's Supper: this is my body which is broken for you; this cup is the new testament in my blood. Of spiritual gifts he showed the more excellent way is charity. He declared the resurrection: Christ rose from the dead the firstfruits, and so in Christ shall all be made alive; death is swallowed up in victory. Thanks be to God, which giveth us the victory through our Lord Jesus Christ.

The Second Epistle of Paul the Apostle to the Corinthians; blessed be God, the Father of mercies, and the God of all comfort, who comforteth us in all our tribulation. He declared that if any man be in Christ, he is a new creature; old things are passed away, behold, all things are become new. God was in Christ, reconciling the world unto Himself, not imputing their trespasses, and hath committed unto us the word of reconciliation. We have this treasure in earthen vessels, that the excellency of the power may be of God; though our outward man perish, yet the inward man is renewed day by day. For the light affliction, which is but for a moment, worketh for us a far more exceeding and eternal weight of glory; while we look not at the things which are seen, but at the things which are not seen. He pleaded for the collection for the poor saints, commended Titus, and said, though we walk in the flesh, we do not war after the flesh.

The Epistle of Paul the Apostle to the Galatians; Paul, an apostle not of men but by Jesus Christ, marvelled that they were so soon removed from Him that called them into the grace of Christ unto another gospel. He recounted how he persecuted the church of God, but was called by God's grace, and conferred not with flesh and blood. We are justified by faith in Christ, and not by the works of the law. As many as are of the works of the law are under the curse; but Christ hath redeemed us from the curse of the law, being made a curse for us. Before faith came we were kept under the law; but when the fulness of the time was come, God sent forth His Son, made of a woman, made under the law, to redeem them that were under the law. Stand fast therefore in the liberty wherewith Christ hath made us free, and be not entangled again with the yoke of bondage. For in Christ Jesus neither circumcision availeth anything, nor uncircumcision, but a new creature.

Paul, an apostle of Jesus Christ, wrote unto the saints at Ephesus and the faithful in Christ Jesus; blessed be the God and Father of our Lord Jesus Christ, who hath blessed us with all spiritual blessings in heavenly places in Christ, according as He hath chosen us in Him before the foundation of the world. We were dead in trespasses and sins, walking according to the course of this world; but God, who is rich in mercy, hath quickened us together with Christ, by grace ye are saved. By grace are ye saved through faith, and that not of yourselves: it is the gift of God. He is our peace, who hath made both one, and hath broken down the middle wall of partition. Now unto Him that is able to do exceeding abundantly above all that we ask or think, according to the power that worketh in us, be glory in the church by Christ Jesus throughout all ages. Put on the whole armour of God, that ye may be able to stand against the wiles of the devil.

Paul and Timotheus, the servants of Jesus Christ, wrote unto all the saints at Philippi; giving thanks for their fellowship in the gospel, and having them in his heart, whether in bonds or in the defence of the gospel. The things which happened unto him fell out rather unto the furtherance of the gospel. He exhorted them to let their moderation be known unto all men, for the Lord is at hand. "Let this mind be in you, which was also in Christ Jesus: who, being in the form of God, thought it not robbery to be equal with God, but made himself of no reputation, and took upon him the form of a servant, and was made in the likeness of men, and being found in fashion as a man, he humbled himself, and became obedient unto death, even the death of the cross." He counted all things but loss for the excellency of the knowledge of Christ Jesus; I press toward the mark for the prize of the high calling of God in Christ Jesus. My God shall supply all your need according to His riches in glory by Christ Jesus.

Paul, an apostle of Jesus Christ, wrote to the saints at Colosse, giving thanks for their faith in Christ Jesus and love to all the saints, since they heard the word of the truth of the gospel, which is come unto you, as it is in all the world. "Who is the image of the invisible God, the firstborn of every creature: for by him were all things created, that are in heaven, and that are in earth, visible and invisible." He is the head of the body, the church: who is the beginning, the firstborn from the dead. He warned them to beware lest any man spoil them through philosophy and vain deceit, after the tradition of men. If ye then be risen with Christ, seek those things which are above, where Christ sitteth on the right hand of God. Let the word of Christ dwell in you richly. And whatsoever ye do in word or deed, do all in the name of the Lord Jesus, giving thanks to God and the Father by him.

Paul and Silvanus and Timotheus wrote unto the church of the Thessalonians, giving thanks always for them, remembering their work of faith, and labour of love, and patience of hope in our Lord Jesus Christ. The gospel came not unto you in word only, but also in power, and in the Holy Ghost, and in much assurance. He exhorteth them to walk as becometh saints, to abstain from fornication, and to love one another. Concerning them which are asleep, he saith, I would not have you ignorant, brethren; for if we believe that Jesus died and rose again, even so them also which sleep in Jesus will God bring with Him. For the Lord himself shall descend from heaven with a shout, with the voice of the archangel, and with the trump of God: and the dead in Christ shall rise first: then we which are alive and remain shall be caught up together with them in the clouds, to meet the Lord in the air: and so shall we ever be with the Lord. Wherefore comfort one another with these words.

The Second Epistle of Paul to the Thessalonians, thanking God for their growing faith and charity, and the patience and faith in all their persecutions. He comforted them concerning the coming of our Lord Jesus Christ, saying, Let no man deceive you by any means; for that day shall not come, except there come a falling away first, and that man of sin be revealed, the son of perdition; who opposeth and exalteth himself above all that is called God, or that is worshipped. And then shall that Wicked be revealed, whom the Lord shall consume with the spirit of his mouth, and shall destroy with the brightness of his coming. Stand fast, and hold the traditions which ye have been taught. The Lord is faithful, who shall stablish you, and keep you from evil. Now we command you, brethren, in the name of our Lord Jesus Christ, that ye withdraw yourselves from every brother that walketh disorderly, and not after the tradition which he received of us.

The First Epistle of Paul the Apostle to Timothy, his own son in the faith; he charged him to abide at Ephesus that he might charge some that they teach no other doctrine, neither give heed to fables and endless genealogies. Now the end of the commandment is charity out of a pure heart. This is a faithful saying, and worthy of all acceptation, that Christ Jesus came into the world to save sinners, of whom I am chief. I will therefore that men pray everywhere, lifting up holy hands, without wrath and doubting. Let the woman learn in silence with all subjection. If a man desire the office of a bishop, he desireth a good work. Let no man despise thy youth; but be thou an example of the believers, in word, in conversation, in charity, in spirit, in faith, in purity. Take heed unto thyself, and unto the doctrine; continue in them: for in doing this thou shalt both save thyself, and them that hear thee.

Paul, an apostle of Jesus Christ, wrote to Timothy, his dearly beloved son, calling to remembrance the unfeigned faith that was in him, which dwelt first in his grandmother Lois, and his mother Eunice. God hath not given us the spirit of fear, but of power, and of love, and of a sound mind. Be not thou therefore ashamed of the testimony of our Lord, nor of me his prisoner. All scripture is given by inspiration of God, and is profitable for doctrine, for reproof, for correction, for instruction in righteousness: that the man of God may be perfect, thoroughly furnished unto all good works. Preach the word; be instant in season, out of season; reprove, rebuke, exhort with all longsuffering and doctrine. I have fought a good fight, I have finished my course, I have kept the faith: henceforth there is laid up for me a crown of righteousness, which the Lord, the righteous judge, shall give me at that day. The Lord Jesus Christ be with thy spirit.

Paul, a servant of God and an apostle of Jesus Christ, wrote to Titus, his own son after the common faith, whom he had left in Crete to set in order the things that were wanting, and to ordain elders in every city, as he had appointed him. An elder must be blameless, the husband of one wife, holding fast the faithful word. The aged men are to be sober, grave, temperate, sound in faith; the aged women likewise are to be in behaviour as becometh holiness, teaching the young women to be sober, to love their husbands, to be keepers at home. For the grace of God that bringeth salvation hath appeared to all men, teaching us that, denying ungodliness and worldly lusts, we should live soberly, righteously, and godly. Not by works of righteousness which we have done, but according to His mercy He saved us, by the washing of regeneration, and renewing of the Holy Ghost; which He shed on us abundantly through Jesus Christ our Saviour. Maintain good works.

Paul, a prisoner of Jesus Christ, wrote unto Philemon his dearly beloved and fellowlabourer, and to the church in his house, giving thanks for his love and faith, that the communication of his faith may become effectual. He besought him for Onesimus, his son, whom he had begotten in his bonds, who in time past was to Philemon unprofitable, but now profitable to him and to Philemon. He sent him again, and charged, receive him not now as a servant, but above a servant, a brother beloved. If he hath wronged thee, or oweth thee ought, put that on mine account; I Paul have written it with mine own hand, I will repay it. Yet I say, having confidence in thy obedience, I wrote unto thee, knowing that thou wilt also do more than I say. The grace of our Lord Jesus Christ be with your spirit.

The Epistle to the Hebrews; God, who at sundry times and in divers manners spake in time past unto the fathers by the prophets, hath in these last days spoken unto us by His Son, whom He hath appointed heir of all things. He is the brightness of His glory and the express image of His person. Being made so much better than the angels, He hath by inheritance obtained a more excellent name. It is impossible for those who were once enlightened to renew them again unto repentance; for it is impossible to please God without faith. Jesus is the author and finisher of our faith; while we look unto Him who for the joy that was set before Him endured the cross, despising the shame, and is set down at the right hand of the throne of God. Now once in the end of the world hath He appeared to put away sin by the sacrifice of Himself. Having therefore, brethren, boldness to enter into the holiest by the blood of Jesus, let us draw near with a true heart in full assurance of faith.

The General Epistle of James, a servant of God and of the Lord Jesus Christ, to the twelve tribes scattered abroad; he exhorteth them to count it all joy when they fall into divers temptations, knowing that the trying of faith worketh patience. If any lack wisdom, let him ask of God, that giveth to all men liberally, and upbraideth not. A double minded man is unstable in all his ways. Let every man be swift to hear, slow to speak, slow to wrath. Be ye doers of the word, and not hearers only, deceiving your own selves. What doth it profit, my brethren, though a man say he hath faith, and have not works? can faith save him? Was not Abraham our father justified by works, when he had offered Isaac his son upon the altar? For as the body without the spirit is dead, so faith without works is dead also. The effectual fervent prayer of a righteous man availeth much; and he which converteth the sinner from the error of his way shall save a soul from death.

The First Epistle General of Peter, an apostle of Jesus Christ, to the strangers scattered throughout Pontus, Galatia, Cappadocia, Asia, and Bithynia, elect according to the foreknowledge of God the Father, through sanctification of the Spirit, unto obedience and sprinkling of the blood of Jesus Christ. Blessed be the God and Father of our Lord Jesus Christ, which according to His abundant mercy hath begotten us again unto a lively hope by the resurrection of Jesus Christ from the dead, to an inheritance incorruptible, undefiled, and that fadeth not away. Wherefore gird up the loins of your mind, be sober, and hope to the end. Ye were not redeemed with corruptible things, as silver and gold, but with the precious blood of Christ, as of a lamb without blemish and without spot. Christ hath once suffered for sins, the just for the unjust, that He might bring us to God. Be clothed with humility, for the elders which are among you I exhort, who am also an elder; when the chief Shepherd shall appear, ye shall receive a crown of glory that fadeth not away.

The Second General Epistle of Peter, to them that have obtained like precious faith; grace and peace be multiplied through the knowledge of God and of Jesus our Lord. His divine power hath given unto us all things that pertain unto life and godliness. Giving all diligence, add to your faith virtue, and to virtue knowledge, and to knowledge temperance, and to temperance patience, and to patience godliness, and to godliness brotherly kindness, and to brotherly kindness charity. There were false prophets also among the people, even as there shall be false teachers among you, who privily shall bring in damnable heresies. The Lord knoweth how to deliver the godly out of temptations, and to reserve the unjust unto the day of judgment to be punished. But the day of the Lord will come as a thief in the night, wherein the heavens shall pass away with a great noise. Grow in grace, and in the knowledge of our Lord and Saviour Jesus Christ.

The First Epistle General of John; that which was from the beginning, which we have heard, which we have seen with our eyes, and our hands have handled, of the Word of life, declare we unto you, that ye also may have fellowship with us. This then is the message which we have heard of Him, and declare unto you, that God is light, and in Him is no darkness at all. If we confess our sins, He is faithful and just to forgive us our sins, and to cleanse us from all unrighteousness. My little children, these things write I unto you, that ye sin not. Whosoever abideth in Him sinneth not; whosoever sinneth hath not seen Him, neither known Him. We know that we have passed from death unto life, because we love the brethren. This is His commandment, That we should believe on the name of His Son Jesus Christ, and love one another. Hereby know we that we dwell in Him, and He in us, because He hath given us of His Spirit.

The Second Epistle of John; the elder wrote unto the elect lady and her children, whom he loved in the truth. He rejoiced greatly that he found of her children walking in truth. He besought her, not as writing a new commandment, but that which we had from the beginning, that we love one another. Many deceivers are entered into the world, who confess not that Jesus Christ is come in the flesh; this is a deceiver and an antichrist. Look to yourselves, that we lose not those things which we have wrought, but that we receive a full reward. Whosoever transgresseth, and abideth not in the doctrine of Christ, hath not God. If there come any unto you, and bring not this doctrine, receive him not into your house, neither bid him God speed. Having many things to write unto you, I would not write with paper and ink; but I trust to come unto you, and speak face to face, that our joy may be full.

The Third Epistle of John; the elder wrote unto the wellbeloved Gaius, whom he loved in the truth, wishing that he mightest prosper and be in health, even as his soul prospereth. He rejoiced greatly when the brethren came and testified of the truth that was in him, even as he walked in the truth. He hath no greater joy than to hear that his children walk in truth. He praised Gaius for faithfully doing whatsoever he did for the brethren and to strangers, who had borne witness of his charity. I wrote unto the church; but Diotrephes, who loveth to have the preeminence among them, receiveth us not, and forbiddeth them that would, and casteth them out of the church. Beloved, follow not that which is evil, but that which is good. Demetrius hath good report of all men, and of the truth itself. Peace be to thee. Our friends salute thee. Greet the friends by name.

The General Epistle of Jude, the servant of Jesus Christ, and brother of James, to them that are sanctified by God the Father and preserved in Jesus Christ; it was needful for him to write unto them, and exhort them that they should earnestly contend for the faith which was once delivered unto the saints. For there are certain men crept in unawares, ungodly men, turning the grace of our God into lasciviousness, and denying the only Lord God, and our Lord Jesus Christ. They are spots in your feasts of charity, clouds without water, trees whose fruit withereth, raging waves of the sea, foaming out their own shame, wandering stars, to whom is reserved the blackness of darkness for ever. Build up yourselves on your most holy faith, praying in the Holy Ghost, and of some have compassion, making a difference; and others save with fear, pulling them out of the fire. Now unto Him that is able to keep you from falling, and to present you faultless before the presence of His glory with exceeding joy, be glory and majesty, dominion and power, both now and ever.

The Revelation of Jesus Christ, which God gave unto Him, to shew unto His servants things which must shortly come to pass, and signified it by His angel unto His servant John, who bare record in Patmos. John saw the Son of man in the midst of the seven candlesticks, and wrote the messages to the seven churches of Asia. He was caught up to heaven and saw the throne, the living creatures, and the Lamb that had been slain, opening the seals; and the four horsemen, the martyrs under the altar, and the great Day of wrath. The seven trumpets sounded, the two witnesses prophesied, and Michael and his angels fought the dragon, which was cast out. The beast rose from the sea and made war with the saints, but the Lamb overcame them, and the angels declared the fall of Babylon. The harvest was reaped, the wrath of God poured out in vials, and the marriage of the Lamb was come. Gog and Magog were consumed with fire from heaven; the dead were judged, death and hell cast into the lake of fire; and John saw the new Jerusalem descending out of heaven from God. He which testified these things saith, Surely I come quickly. Even so, come, Lord Jesus. The grace of our Lord Jesus Christ be with you all. Amen.
